methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: QuantumScape
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: LanzaTech
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $800M+

data.narrative:
Solid Power, a publicly traded solid-state battery developer in the Climate & Energy sector, has raised $232M to date. The valuation universe for this stage and subsector is sparse: only 1 public comparable (LanzaTech, ticker LNZA) and 8 private peers including QuantumScape, TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, and Helion. This narrow peer set reflects the early-stage, capital-intensive nature of advanced battery technology.

Peer-median valuation methodology is not applicable here. A robust comparable-company analysis requires a minimum of three datapoints within the same stage and valuation tier; the Climate & Energy sector contains 21 same-sector peers overall, but filtering for identical stage, adjacent development phase, and similar capital tier yields fewer than three credible matches. Expanding the peer set to all 21 same-sector companies would introduce substantial methodological noise given their heterogeneous business models, geographies, and technology readiness levels.

Without sufficient narrowly-defined comparables, triangulation across revenue multiples, exit precedent, or discounted cash flow would rest on weak foundations. This data gap reflects a market reality: solid-state battery companies operate at a unique intersection of unproven commercial scale, long development timelines, and bifurcated funding sources (corporate strategic, venture, and public markets). Any valuation assignment should acknowledge that reliance on LNZA alone as a public anchor introduces single-name basis risk and does not constitute a defensible analysis.
